region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely served in Singapore Mamak establishments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for aloo gosht; 'meat curry (food product)' is the closest defensible parent class. Specific lamb-and-potato curry term absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers meat-based curry dishes broadly; no specific aloo gosht node exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of aloo gosht is approximately 250 g, representing one bowl of curry with a mix of mutton pieces and potato chunks in spiced gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Gosht (Curry, mutton, with potato and Indian spice) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06060001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and provenance 'canonical'. All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked lamb shoulder stew (NDB 17290) proportioned to the HPB total fat value of 6.5 g/100g; approximately 43% of fat in cooked lamb stew is saturated, yielding ~2.8 g/100g saturated fat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aloo gosht in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-potato curry entries. Cooked potato in a curry context has a substantially lower GI than plain boiled potato (~78) due to the fat, protein, and fibre matrix of the dish, which slows gastric emptying. The protein content (17.8 g/100g) and fat content (6.5 g/100g) further suppress the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 4.7 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.7 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 833 mg sodium, representing 36% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer, request preparation with reduced added salt, avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes such as salted fish or soy-sauce-based sides, and consider home preparation using low-sodium stock and potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 4.7 g/100g and sugar 0.7 g/100g are low; high protein (17.8 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-prandial glucose rise; estimated GI is low (~40).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aloo gosht is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (150–200 g); pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice to keep the overall meal glycaemic load low, and request a lower potato-to-meat ratio to further reduce starchy carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100g from lamb; sodium 333 mg/100g; red meat consumption is associated with elevated LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular risk when consumed frequently.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit aloo gosht to occasional consumption (once per week or less), choose lean cuts of lamb with visible fat trimmed, keep portions to 150 g, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables and whole grains to mitigate the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato) may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit aloo gosht; if consumed, request a preparation with reduced lamb quantity, increased turmeric and ginger (which provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit), and substitute potato with a non-nightshade vegetable such as carrot or zucchini.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo gosht (Hindi: आलू गोश्त; 'aloo' = potato, 'gosht' = meat/mutton) is a slow-cooked South Asian curry made with bone-in or boneless mutton (lamb) and potato chunks simmered in a richly spiced gravy of onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Singapore's Mamak (Tamil Muslim) food establishments, where it is a staple curry alongside roti canai, naan, or plain rice. The texture is tender and falling-off-the-bone for the meat, with soft potato absorbing the aromatic gravy. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, mildly spicy, and fragrant from whole spices. Nutritionally, aloo gosht is high in protein (17.8 g/100g), moderate in fat (6.5 g/100g), and low in carbohydrate (4.7 g/100g), making it a relatively low-calorie curry at 149 kcal/100g. Sodium is elevated at 333 mg/100g, which is a concern for hypertension. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low GI (~40) and high protein content, but should be consumed cautiously by individuals with hypertension (high sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from lamb), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid from red meat and nightshade vegetables). Iron content is notable at 1.9 mg/100g, contributing to dietary iron intake from red meat haem iron.
common_user_queries: Is aloo gosht suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record (cr_id F06060001, confidence 0.95, provenance 'canonical', review_status 'reviewed'). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked lamb stew data as a defensible secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific aloo gosht preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry or USDA FoodData Central as a directly matching composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 17290 (cooked lamb shoulder, stewed) proportioned to the HPB total fat value; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for aloo gosht exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input record has been removed as aloo gosht contains potatoes and 4.7 g/100g carbohydrate, which is inconsistent with strict ketogenic dietary criteria. The input 'low_calorie' tag is retained as a contextual note only; at 149 kcal/100g the dish is moderate-to-low calorie relative to other curries but not formally classified as a low-calorie food product.
